html:
DoubleDown Interactive (DDI.F / DDI US)

DoubleDown is now less a normal listed gaming stock than a control-transaction value fight around a cash-rich, highly profitable social casino business. The market price is already above DoubleU Games’ $11.25 per ADS take-private proposal, which is the right market reaction: the bid looks like an attempt by the 67.1% controlling shareholder to buy a growing cash machine for close to the cash on its own balance sheet.

The operating facts changed meaningfully before the governance fight appeared. Q1 2026 revenue rose 12.7% to $94.1 million, adjusted EBITDA rose 24.0% to $38.2 million, and profit attributable to DoubleDown rose 48.4% to $35.4 million, or $0.71 per ADS. Operating cash flow was $46.4 million in one quarter. The company ended March with $432.8 million of cash, $100.6 million of short-term investments, and only $33.0 million of related-party borrowings. That is roughly $10 per ADS of net cash against a $12.14 U.S. ADS price, or about €10.44 for DDI.F at EUR/USD near 1.163.

The variant perception is not simply “net cash stock cheap.” That is too lazy. The real point is that the business attached to the cash is still producing current earnings power of roughly $2.40 to $2.80 per ADS, depending on how much Q1 finance income and working-capital timing one normalizes away. Even on the whole market cap, the stock is only about 4.3x annualized Q1 EPS and roughly 5x a more conservative forward earnings base. On enterprise value, the operating business is priced at under 1x run-rate EBITDA and likely around 1x normalized free cash flow. That is not a stale trailing multiple applied to a shrinking app. It is a forward multiple on a business that just reported higher users, higher payer conversion, higher ARPDAU, and higher cash generation.

The old bearish label was “melting social casino asset with stranded Korean cash.” That was defensible when DoubleDown Casino was the overwhelming driver and revenue was flat. It is less defensible now. The company has added SuprNation in European real-money iGaming, acquired Germany’s WHOW Games in July 2025, and pushed direct-to-consumer payment channels hard. In Q1, social casino revenue grew 9.5%, SuprNation grew 30.0%, and DTC revenue rose to $34.0 million, 44.2% of social casino revenue versus 12.8% a year earlier. DTC matters because it reduces dependence on Apple and Google toll roads and improves the durability of margins. WHOW lowers average monthly revenue per payer, but it raises payer conversion and broadens the revenue base into Germany. That is a business mix transition, not a clean single-product decline.

The most important new catalyst is the lowball take-private proposal. DoubleU offered $11.25 per ADS for the public stake, valuing the minority at about $184 million. The proposal itself admits DoubleU wants to unify operations and consolidate DoubleDown’s financial resources. In plain English, the parent sees exactly what public holders should see: the cash pile, the free cash flow, the DTC channel shift, and the value of collapsing public-company costs. A top-10 holder, Four Tree Island, publicly urged the special committee to reject the offer, arguing that the proposal assigns negligible value to the operating business. That criticism is directionally correct. A $11.25 sale would let the parent capture most of the upside just as the numbers inflect.

The deal mechanics also matter. DoubleU says it owns 67.1%, but its own proposal requires 95% approval and a majority of unaffiliated votes cast. With the stock trading at $12.14, public holders are effectively saying $11.25 is not enough. This is not a normal merger-arb spread where the market waits below a binding bid. It is a controlled-company negotiation where the special committee has leverage because the bid is visibly stale only weeks after Q1 results.

The base case is not that DDI becomes a high-multiple growth gaming company. The base case is simpler: the special committee either forces a meaningfully higher deal or the stock remains public with a much harder spotlight on cash returns. If the company can produce $120 million to $140 million of normalized annual free cash flow and carries around $500 million of net cash, a $900 million to $1.0 billion equity value is not aggressive. That is still only about 7x to 8x forward earnings and 2.5x to 3.5x EV/EBITDA after cash. A fair take-private should be much closer to $18 to $20 than $11.25.

The bull case is that Q1 was not a one-off. DTC keeps rising, SuprNation’s Los Vegas brand continues to scale, WHOW gives DoubleDown a better European social casino platform, and the parent has to pay a real control premium. At $25 per ADS, the company would still be valued at roughly 9x to 10x forward earnings, with the operating business at a mid-single-digit EV/EBITDA multiple after cash. That would not be expensive for a 40% EBITDA margin digital gaming business with nearly zero capex, a larger DTC channel, and a proven ability to convert profit into cash.

The risk is governance. The controlling shareholder may try to grind the special committee and minority holders toward a mediocre deal. If the offer is withdrawn, the stock could temporarily fall back toward pre-bid levels because the public float is small, social casino is unpopular, and many screens still treat DDI as a dead-money cash box. Regulation is also real: social casino and iGaming models can attract legal and platform scrutiny. The business still depends heavily on a narrow set of casino-style gaming franchises, and SuprNation’s 2025 goodwill impairment is a reminder that acquisitions can disappoint. Idle cash is not automatically good capital allocation. If management refuses both a fair sale and a serious cash return, the discount can persist.

But the asymmetry is unusually clean. The current price is only modestly above the inadequate bid, while the operating business is cheap even without giving full credit for cash. Q1 2026 turned the story from “cash-rich decline” into “cash-rich, cash-generative inflection with a conflicted controller trying to buy the minority before the rerating.” That is exactly the kind of ignored, ugly, wrong-label small-cap setup where forward valuation matters more than historical multiple screens.

Targets are per ADS, with DDI.F EUR equivalents estimated at EUR/USD 1.163. Current price: $12.14, about €10.44.

Bear: $9.50, about €8.17, -22%. The deal fails, governance remains poor, Q1 growth fades, and the market again values DDI as a controlled cash box with a melting operating business.

Base: $19.00, about €16.34, +57%. The special committee forces a fairer take-private or the public market rerates the stock to roughly 7x to 8x forward earnings while still applying a heavy control discount.

Bull: $27.00, about €23.22, +122%. Growth persists, DTC mix keeps improving, cash is returned or paid for in a proper control transaction, and the operating business receives even a modest mid-single-digit EBITDA multiple.
